name: Beth

parsed_profile.persona: Linda is an older working lawyer who has lived in the Berkeley Hills for many years. She has an extensive garden that she is deeply invested in and has evacuated once before though no fire occurred. She approaches fire mitigation compliance pragmatically but with frustration about inconsistent messaging from young, inexperienced inspectors and the lack of action by regional authorities to create firebreaks in the parks. She is relatively compliant and willing to spend significant money on mitigation, but questions why homeowners bear the entire burden when the city and regional parks control the most dangerous areas. She values her time over money at this stage of life and relies heavily on neighbor recommendations rather than getting multiple contractor estimates.

insurance_non_renewal.context: When asked to share which insurance company she was with and discuss her insurance situation, given that her insurance was renewed while neighbors faced cancellations or massive rate increases.

defensible_space_inspection.real_response: There is built into the system a lot of inconsistency.
defensible_space_inspection.context: After describing how the fire inspectors were young interns without experience, how different inspectors gave different advice, and how the volunteer district heads also provided inconsistent guidance despite trying hard to create consistency.

neighbor_pressure.real_response: I got his name from my neighbors across the street. My time is much more valuable to me than the money.
neighbor_pressure.context: When asked how she found the contractor Nigel who may have overcharged, explaining that she relied on neighbor recommendations because she values time over money at this point in her life.

firewise_outreach.real_response: There have been so many meetings. There's been web meetings, and I haven't gone to any of them. I plan to go to one, and I never managed to enjoy it. It's like you have your life and you don't want to spend all this time.
firewise_outreach.context: When discussing the numerous community meetings held at the local church and online about fire safety and compliance.

resources_assistance.real_response: The Berkeley fire department said that if you're over 65, I think they would do some of that removal for you. They offered doing that to us, and I didn't. I just I have a gardener, and we've had the gardener take some of this stuff.
resources_assistance.context: When mentioning that the city offered vegetation removal assistance to seniors but she chose to use her own gardener instead.

memory_seeds.description: I live right at the top of the Berkeley Hills, on the side near the park.
I was a graduate student at Berkeley for 11 years, first in English and then in law school, and lived on five thousand dollars a year during those years.
I already took out a bunch of bushes that are within five feet of the house, and there's more to take out.
We have two Japanese maple trees coming through decks right next to our house.
Two Fire Marshals from the Berkeley fire department came to our house to talk about what we would need to do to satisfy the Berkeley law supplementing the state law.
The fire marshals told us, surprisingly, that trees don't burn down because it's very hard to catch a tree trunk on fire due to water coming through the trunk, even if the fire gets very hot.
They said we have to avoid having the leaves at the top overhang the roof, so we had all our trees trimmed with enough space so that if foliage burns up, it's not setting the roof on fire.

The people who came out to give us advice were between like 20 and 24 years old, which indicated to all of us that they hadn't had a lot of experience with any of this—they were basically sending interns out.
There are volunteer heads of districts up here who head firewise groups, like community groups for earthquake preparedness, and they try very hard to create consistency about what people need to do.
We had a guy who was actually an engineer who was the head of one of these groups, and he was telling us a lot of stuff.
We have a deadline of June 1st to get the first level of stuff done, but then there's going to be more done sequentially with more areas affected.
On the side of our house we have a very large silk tassel, a native plant that's been grown for 40 years and covers the whole side of the house on a trellis.

The fire guys said they're not sure they'll make us take the silk tassel out—I might try to get away with it if I clear all the foliage up to four feet and make sure it's not right next to windows, because that's how fires get pulled inside houses.
I'm not gonna cut down the silk tassel until they come again.
The Berkeley fire department gave us materials for free—metal mesh netting—and they changed every single gutter on our entire house from plastic filters to metal mesh.
They said the metal mesh really keeps everything out and prevents debris in gutters from catching fire, while plastic mesh will burn up instantaneously.
We were notified the fire department would give these materials for free, but my husband went three or four times and every time he made an appointment, nobody was there to give it to him.
We finally got the materials and then hired a guy named Nigel who put the mesh on the gutters and covered every hole that went into the house with that stuff so Embers couldn't go inside.
Nigel put mesh on all the fireplace vents and heating vents and all vents that come out on the roof so no embers could go down into the house.
I know other people who did something similar and paid something similar for getting that done—this is a very expensive process for people to comply.
We were advised that when fires travel on parallel fences and cross gates, that's when houses burn down, so they recommended we put in metal gates instead of wooden gates.
We have already paid for half of this process—we've ordered four metal gates total, which will cost us fifteen thousand dollars to put in.

One option is to just take the gates off and leave the yard open, but we have deer in this area and if I take those gates off, I can forget a garden—deer eat absolutely everything, even poison plants.
Another option is to leave the gates open when evacuating, but not every evacuation equals a fire, and that would still allow deer to come in and destroy the garden I've spent years on.
I actually evacuated once, stayed at my daughter's house on Bancroft near Sacramento, and we took our parrot and dog, but neither time was there a fire.
We're up to just about twenty thousand dollars for complying with the requirements.
I'm still a working lawyer and not retired, so my time is much more valuable to me than the money at this point in my life.
I really liked Nigel and thought he did a good job, but if I were to do it again, I would ask why he's charging this amount.
I'm a really avid gardener and we had most of this information last summer, but I knew I didn't want to take anything out or move anything until the rain started because plants would die in summer.
We've successfully moved five or six different plants, and it's kind of like redoing your kitchen—there's a lot of steps and people and things not being done right, then doing it again.
It took us three months from the time they told us we could pick up materials from the Berkeley fire department until we actually were able to get them.
The gates we ordered were supposed to come in February and they haven't come yet, but the small family company was honest—their business has probably multiplied by 25 times.
I used a company called Juice because my neighbors used them and they did a good job, and I saw the work done—I hate getting lots of estimates because it's a time suck.
We had a bush right next to our house and had a bunch of it taken out yesterday.
I'm not sure why someone's calling me from an unknown number.
The fire danger at our house is totally obvious, but it's the same for houses two blocks below us—fires usually come up the hill from the bay winds, not down, except on rare days with easterly winds.
If you go to the engineering building up on Euclid and Hearst, the danger to all those houses is really the same as at our house—they're just picking on us because we're near the park.
More than one of my neighbors has asked why they don't have a wildfire break at the park, since the state and region and city control that non-inhabited area.
The fire in 1991 jumped the freeway—everyone thought it would stop at the freeway, which was maybe 100 feet wide of asphalt and metal, but it jumped it.
The fire supposedly went up to between 2000 and 2500 degrees—like a fire in a kiln to fire a ceramic, not like a birthday cake candle.
If they made a fire break 200 feet wide at Wildcat Canyon, which is already 50 feet wide, we would not be at risk for fire anymore than someone at Willard Park.
They've said they are not going to do that fire break because there's certain plants protected by this or that rule.
I'm relatively compliant and will do it, but you can't help but say this is ridiculous—they have the power to completely create a fire break with East Bay Regional Parks and they're not.
The university has taken down thousands of trees—you can see all those buildings you couldn't see before—but not the city, and not the state, and not the regional parks.
Why should I be spending twenty thousand dollars when the city of Berkeley isn't clearing the trees in an area that they have jurisdiction over?

My neighbor says the insurance is really the bottom line, because Berkeley probably won't charge penalties if you don't do everything, but if you get your insurance terminated, that's the ultimate decision.
My neighbors across the street literally complied because their insurance had been canceled.
Our insurance company renewed our insurance and told us our house had a very low chance of burning down.
Someone else on the block was told that as soon as they said 94708, the insurance company said absolutely not, they don't insure houses in that zip code.
Another insurance company raised someone's rate from ten thousand to seventeen thousand dollars a year on the adjoining block and said they needed to take certain mitigation steps.
Some insurance companies have programs where they load in the house details and send drones over everybody's houses to make judgments about whether your house is trouble or not.
As far as I can tell, the results from insurance companies couldn't be more variable—everyone is getting a different result, some terminated, some told to go to the fair plan.
Nobody has confidence the fair plan is actually going to cover fire because it's spread among so many people, and every time you renew they add another twenty-five hundred dollars.

While we might save 10 to 15 percent on debris removal by working as a neighborhood, insurance companies are raising rates from eight thousand to twelve thousand dollars a year—that's four thousand dollars.
Our old house was eight feet away from our neighbor, and it's very common in the hills to have a house worth five million dollars be eight feet away from the neighbor.
We're 50 feet from the house next door and 20 feet from the other house, which is very unusual in the Berkeley Hills.

name: Charles

parsed_profile.persona: Robert is a longtime Berkeley Hills homeowner who has lived on Preston Road at the top of the hill for over 40 years. He is a retired consultant who worked with major insurance companies helping them quantify risk, which gives him a sophisticated understanding of fire hazard from both technical and industry perspectives. He is pragmatic and compliance-oriented toward the Ember ordinance, viewing defensible space work as analogous to getting his car smogged—an unpleasant necessity mandated by law. He is frustrated by neighbors he perceives as selfish or in denial, comparing their resistance to tobacco industry science denial tactics. He trusts his own technical judgment and has taken proactive steps, though he acknowledges inspectors may require more.

insurance_non_renewal.real_response: It's only prudent what they're doing, and I'm totally understand that, um. But, you know, on the other hand, we homeowners are. Left holes in the bag literally.
insurance_non_renewal.context: When his insurance company of 40 years declined to renew his policy a few years ago, citing high fire risk and wood siding. He recently had to get new insurance through the FAIR Plan after his second insurer also non-renewed.

defensible_space_inspection.real_response: This is like getting my car smogged, you know, I have to go down. I get my car checked for smog every two years, yeah. Um, or I have to draw. I have to stop a red light. But because that's what the law tells me.
defensible_space_inspection.context: When explaining his general attitude toward compliance with defensible space requirements and the Ember ordinance.

neighbor_pressure.real_response: Some of them, I think, are. We're not acting in a in a neighborly manner.
neighbor_pressure.context: When discussing neighbors on his block who have been reluctant to clear vegetation despite the shared fire risk.

resources_assistance.real_response: I mean, it's actually a great deal, so they're free.
resources_assistance.context: When describing the City of Berkeley's program offering free high-quality aluminum gutter screens to homeowners to prevent ember ignition.

memory_seeds.description: I had not seen an inspection for some time, though I received correspondence from the fire department saying there would be one later in the summer.
About a year ago, a fire officer walked down the street inspecting every house. I bumped into him as he was looking at my house and we talked briefly. He did not enter the property.
My insurance company dropped me after I had been a customer for 40 years. They constantly assured me I was in good hands, and then just said they were discontinuing the policy.
The first insurance company that dropped me gave no explanation beyond saying I was in a high fire risk area and had wood siding.
I reinsured with another company after being dropped, but this year they also declined to renew.
Just in the last few weeks I had to run around and get new insurance. What I've got now is through the FAIR Plan.
The FAIR Plan apparently sent out an inspector within the last couple weeks. I did not see them, but got a notice that they inspected the property.
The house has wood siding, and to replace that would be a very significant expense. It would not make the house fireproof anyway, because there's no such thing as fireproof.
For many years I was a consultant to the largest insurance companies in the world and helped them improve their methods for quantifying risk.
I know where the insurance companies are coming from. They lost billions of dollars in Eaton and Palisades, and before that in Santa Rosa and Paradise.

The truth is that in this area, houses are typically set only feet apart. If one house catches fire, it will ignite the other houses regardless of materials. Stucco might take a few minutes longer than wood, but they'll still burn.
The real issue is preventing the fire in the first place by managing vegetation on our properties and especially the vegetation in Tilden Park.
Tilden Park is now apparently doing something, but they have not been very proactive. They really need to clear a buffer zone along Wildcat Canyon Road, and they don't.

On high fire danger days, the Berkeley and other fire departments need to have fire engines pre-positioned up here. I recall two or three years ago seeing engines pre-positioned near the Tilden Golf Course during high fire season.
I've done a fair amount of vegetation clearance. The inspector will probably want me to do some more, but I've done quite a bit.
This is just like measles and herd immunity. You need everybody to be inoculated. Otherwise you will have an outbreak. Same thing with houses—all the houses have to comply with the Ember program.
Once a wildfire gets going, unless the fire department is right there, it's too late.

I think the bottom line is that some people are selfish. They want to cling to their garden, which I sympathize with, but if you have gangrene in your leg and the doctor says he has to amputate, you have a choice: lose your leg or lose your life. Same with your garden—lose your garden or lose your house. And you're threatening everybody else's house.

Some neighbors use the same tactic as the tobacco industry—science denial. They say things like 'if we pull out the vegetation we may get landslides,' even though we're at the top of the hill and the rainwater already flows into the ground.
I think it's inertia, apathy, resistance to being told what to do—which is a very strong trait in the United States—and general argumentativeness.
On our block, I would say probably 20 or 30 percent are like me and say 'you gotta do this, end of story.' The rest of the people will do it when they have to.

There's a woman on our block named Cindy who is very vocally opposed to the Ember ordinance. I don't know her personally, but I've followed her arguments and she grasps at every possible reason to oppose it.

We have a gardener who comes in a couple times a month. I had them do the vegetation clearance work, and it cost me a few hundred dollars—maybe five hundred to a thousand—to take out quite a few plants and replace them with red lava rock.

The gardener lives on the block, only a mile and a half away. I gave him instructions based on the Ember brochure. I don't think he's taken any special courses, he just does what the property owner tells him to do.
Rain gutters are a weak point for fire because they fill with leaves and other flammable materials. In a wildfire, embers can fall into the gutters, ignite the dry leaves, and set the roof on fire.

The City of Berkeley gave away free high-quality aluminum gutter screens to any homeowner. As soon as I heard about it, I ran down there and got all the screens I needed, which would have cost a few hundred dollars.
I had the gardener install the gutter screens. It took two guys one hour. That should not be more than about one hundred or at most two hundred dollars in labor.
I can see where some contractor comes in and says 'that's a big job, it's going to cost you a thousand bucks.' People like that exist who take advantage of naive homeowners.

Zone one and zone two are irrelevant here. There's not many houses in Berkeley that are more than thirty feet away from another house. Almost none are more than one hundred feet away. So zone zero is what you need to focus on.

The zone system comes out of wildland protection programs. It's appropriate for the wildlands, but in a wildland-urban interface where houses are ten feet apart, there's no proper guidance. People are adapting what was used for the wildlands.
I tried to access the Fireside platform after getting an email about it, but I needed an access code that I don't think they gave me.

name: Edward

parsed_profile.persona: Walter is a homeowner over 65 living on a large half-acre lot on the east side of the Berkeley Hills with underground electricity lines except at his property. He has been actively clearing yard debris as a form of entertainment long before the fire department's involvement, aware of the 1923 Berkeley fire legend and increasing fire risk conditions. He has a cynical, sardonic view of Berkeley governance, noting the city excels at making rules but not enforcing them, though he acknowledges Ember is unusual in actually trying to enforce regulations. He is critical of what he calls 'fire science' when regulatory constraints like CEQA protections for live oak trees and tree exemptions allow real fire hazards like dead wood to remain untouched. Despite his skepticism about the consistency and scientific basis of the regulations, he qualified for and participated in the city's Residential Assistance Program (RAP), having the required defensible space work done by city-contracted crews at no personal cost.

defensible_space_inspection.real_response: The assertion that 'this is fire science' is a stretch at best and laughable at worst.
defensible_space_inspection.context: Reflecting on the Ember program's defensible space requirements and the justifications provided by officials

resources_assistance.real_response: The contractors were very good, very reasonable, qualified professional people. Did the job, cleaned up well and all the rest of it.
resources_assistance.context: Describing the RAP program contractors who performed defensible space work at his property

memory_seeds.description: I have a big yard on the east side of the Hills, looking away from the bay.
I sent photos showing what the Ember people or the fire department wanted me to do.
The first photos showed big brown areas of no vegetation immediately next to the house where there used to be ivy.
They said the ivy itself isn't flammable, but it collects a lot of dead leaves underneath the live vines, which is why they wanted to clear it out.
There were some bushes near the walkway to the front door and living room windows that have been removed.
I saw one skinny tree and a big bush that was way cut back—it used to be much closer to the house.
I removed about two-thirds of the bush before they came; they removed the rest of it.
There was a giant carnation bush in front of the window right up against the house that got cut.
One of the chief complexities in Ember is the definition of the word 'tree'—you might think you know what a tree is, but you don't.
A tree is anything that you call a tree, like any other word.
Because there was a lot of pushback from homeowners when this was first announced, they created the tree exemption.
I have a little tree in front that I told them not to remove because it's a tree.
I used to call what's now seven or eight long trunk-like objects with foliage at the top a 'bush' because it was bushy and abutted the railing of the deck.
There's now about a two-foot space between the deck railing and what we are now calling trees.
When one of the children from the fire department referred to them as trees and asked 'Well, what about these trees?', my eyes lit up and I said I would trim them back.
She said 'Oh, how delightful' when I offered to trim the trees back.
There was some ivy underneath which they removed, and at the bottom of the trees there was a little more ivy and some juniper.
Juniper is like the work of the devil—it's as bad as eucalyptus, very flammable, so they took that.
CEQA is the California Environmental Quality Act, and under it live oak trees are declared sacred—fire crews can't do anything to a live oak tree as far as I understand.
My live oak tree is a hundred years old and has a lot of dead wood on it.
In any definition of fire science, dead wood on a tree would be something you'd remove, but according to CEQA you can't.
All public policy programs are politically constrained one way or another.

There's all this effort being put into dealing with certain legitimate fire hazards, and then other ones that are real—well, you can't deal with them because of the tree exemption, CEQA, and other rules.
Berkeley is excellent at one thing, which is making rules, and not at another thing, which is enforcing rules—we do not enforce rules here.
What's rare about Ember is that they're actually trying to enforce it.
I did not have to pay for the work shown in the pictures.
The city has a program called RAP—Residential Assistance Program—and they've gotten money to pay contractors to do this if you qualify.
My next-door neighbor has two young children and they're too young, so they don't qualify for RAP.
I'm over 65, and there are several other criteria, so I could fill out this little DocuSign form and qualified.
There was a woman who worked with five people—four Spanish-speaking gentlemen did all the work, and her job was to talk to the owner.
I have a very large lot—a half-acre, which is a lot—so I'm not cleaning yard debris all the time, but it's my form of entertainment.
I have done a lot of this yard work before the fire department even came, because of the legend of the 1923 Berkeley fire that started in the east and came down the hill.
That 1923 fire is what's behind all of this—it happened in the past, conditions are getting worse, so the risk is up there.
On my street, the electricity lines are underground except for my house and a couple of others.
I have a power pole at one corner of the lot due to some history that's complicated to explain.

name: Isabelle G

parsed_profile.persona: Claire is a Berkeley Hills homeowner who lives with her partner in a small 1600 square foot wood-sided house near Wildcat Canyon. They have lived there for decades and have State Farm insurance for 40 years. She and her partner completed extensive wildfire mitigation work starting in early 2024, well before the Ember ordinance became law, hiring contractor Kazoo to do defensible space and home hardening including enclosing eaves and installing tempered double-pane windows. Claire is highly engaged in wildfire safety, pragmatic about costs, and frustrated by neighbors' reluctance to act. She sees mitigation as a necessary cost of living in the hills today, comparable to insurance, and believes leadership by example is key to community action.

insurance_non_renewal.real_response: And like Isabel said, that really kind of shocked us. And then we realized that, you know, that this was happening.
insurance_non_renewal.context: Their neighbor to the west lost her home insurance because the house had wood siding, even though it was just one property receiving the non-renewal call. This neighbor's sudden loss of coverage prompted Claire and her partner to realize insurance drops were actively happening in their area.

defensible_space_inspection.real_response: One of the inspectors said we had the best home hardened house in the hills.
defensible_space_inspection.context: During their Ember inspection, which took place on the day of the interview, the inspectors praised their mitigation work after they passed with flying colors.

neighbor_pressure.real_response: Except for all our neighbors think we're really crazy. I mean, they're like, what are you doing? And, you know, that was before it became law, of course.
neighbor_pressure.context: When Claire and her partner removed plants and completed mitigation work before the Ember ordinance was in effect, neighbors questioned what they were doing and thought their actions were excessive.

firewise_outreach.real_response: I think we have a weak firewise group, whereas Michelle's group is just amazing with what they accomplish.
firewise_outreach.context: Claire compared her own Firewise group within Berkeley Woods to Michelle's group, expressing disappointment that their leader hadn't completed mitigation work herself and that members weren't actively engaging or documenting their work.

resources_assistance.real_response: We totally lucked out, you know, because nobody else had done this work or started this work. You know, she was free.

memory_seeds.description: I started wildfire mitigation work two years ago, well before the Firewise group had started getting together about a little over a year ago, but we were already finished by that point.
I hired contractor Kazoo in February or March of 2024 after our neighbor lost her home insurance.
My neighbor to the west received a call that her insurance was not being renewed because her house had wood siding, even though our house also has wood siding.
We have State Farm insurance and have had them for 40 years with no claims.
State Farm recently restarted but dropped half the zip code in Berkeley and half of Orinda to reduce their risk, basically unilaterally by zip code without using intelligence.
We live across the street from Tilden Park in Wildcat Canyon, which is presumably where the wildfire will come through.
We live very close to both our neighbors on a quarter acre lot, which is a very big risk and just the nature of the Berkeley hills.
The insurance company had concerns about some trees over us using satellite view and asked us to clear them back, which we did because we were already planning to do it.
State Farm has a discount available but one of the ten items is no wood siding, so we don't actually qualify even though we did mitigation work.
We went and saw our agent several times in his office and sent him photographs of everything we did, which may have helped us keep our insurance.
Kazoo came and did a full evaluation of what needed to be done and charged us for the evaluation, which was fair, and we got a large prioritized list of everything that needed to be done.
Kazoo started work a few weeks after the evaluation and worked for several months with a group of about four, five, or six people.
One of the larger items was enclosing the eaves underneath the roof, which were open and catch embers, and that was a big job and one of the higher priority items.
The last plant we took out was a big camellia in back where birds would have their nests, and it was absolutely beautiful, and Steve cried when we removed it.
We removed most of the plants ourselves around the house, although we did hire a different contractor to help as well.

Now that the plants are gone and you can see how light it is, I actually really like it, and we just saw a wildcat walk by our house yesterday, which we would never have seen before with all the greenery.
We have no regrets at all about doing the mitigation work and we're very happy we did it.
I think the number one complaint from people is cost, that it's too much money and they shouldn't have to pay for it, but there's residential assistance and financing available.
The number two complaint is the plants and the environmental impact, with people thinking we're destroying the planet, but it's really not, it's just five feet.
We moved some camellias that were close to the house and put them in pots.
I see the cost as part of living today, like having insurance, and we actually do earthquake insurance too, which not everybody does, as the cost of living today to be safe.
We had our windows done last May with tempered double-pane glass, which was very expensive but needed to be done, and we've gotten some benefit with lower cooling and heating costs.
Single pane windows will break at a much lower temperature and then open up so embers can come inside the house during a storm of embers.
The house is much quieter now with the new windows and the heat stays in a little bit longer.
It's virtually impossible to sell your house these days as it is, with prices stabilized if not gone down, but our mitigation work is an investment and the house would sell faster if we wanted to.
There's a whole disclosure for fire compliance required by Alameda County, and if you haven't done things you have to disclose it.
We had our Ember inspection today and passed with flying colors.
People started saying mitigation was too expensive before they even had estimates, and I know that for a fact, and these are people that drive really nice cars and go on European vacations.
I think people's reluctance is not the money, it's their attachment to the plants.
Everybody in the hills can afford a few thousand dollars, and if they can't, there's help of $3,500 available if income is under a hundred thousand dollars.
The fire department has done a really great job going around to different homes giving ideas and showing what they have to do, and they're always available.

Michelle, one of the Firewise leaders, has been sharing photographs of what his home looks like and his group has been helping older residents with clearing brush, bringing students from Berkeley one time.
People have until June to do the mitigation work, and the fire department is being very low key with no pressure, holding people's hands and being reasonable.
The fire department doesn't want this to become confrontational or threatening, they want to completely avoid that.
Once you start working on one side of your home, you realize you can do this and it becomes easier to do the rest, like going on a diet.

Chief Winaker said in an interview that the most important thing is for the leaders of Firewise to do the work themselves so people see the leader as a model, but our Firewise leader hasn't done the work herself.
Our umbrella Firewise group is called Berkeley Woods and within it there are three sub Firewise groups for different streets, making it a large group in the hills.
People in our Firewise group don't really engage, and they're supposed to write up work hours and money spent on a shared worksheet monthly or regularly but people don't.

We had an open house here last April with about 100 people in our courtyard, and Kazoo spoke for an hour and a half about home hardening, with city council member Grant Blackaby and a fire chief attending.
A real estate agent I know says that if anything's going to get people to do this work, it's the insurance, nothing else will get them.
Grant Blackaby has been such a great leader on wildfire mitigation and I respect him, but he's been under so much pressure from people that are really against Ember.

On Next Door, a social network, Grant Blackaby was getting attacked by a small group of anti-Ember people, and one woman even filled out a recall petition against him which was rejected because it was too many words.

The woman who tried to recall Grant Blackaby is a renter who's very eloquent at first but starts spouting fiction about there being no science to support Ember, and there are similarities between this and anti-vaxxers.

I went to several state level resource meetings in Sacramento and testified about Zone Zero, and the state postponed publishing statewide rules until March at the earliest after missing their deadline.

There was a strong contingency in Los Angeles and Westwood that really don't want to do Zone Zero at all, and they were able to stop statewide rules because they're great financial supporters of Governor Newsom.
Berkeley is really the model for the state, and Michelle has been the leader bar none in getting us up to Sacramento and sending letters and being very active.
The neighbor next door met with Kazoo and she's doing some of the screens covering vents, so people are moving forward.
Our neighbor here is finally replacing the five foot wood segment of her fence, which is very important because that's the part that'll act as a wick for the fire.
I'm a little more optimistic these days that people are getting the message and we can have conversations with them and feel good about it rather than feel like we're pestering them.

insurance_non_renewal.real_response: It's very unfair, and it feels capricious. And I think it adds. It probably adds to, um. A feeling that I don't know how prevalent, but I've heard this feedback. Um, a feeling that? Like, none of this will do any good.

neighbor_pressure.real_response: Last year, there was so much polarization. Opinions like people being? Like, really, really, gung-ho, and you're wrong if you aren't into fire safety and then other people equally. Passionate about. You know, this is gonna be terrible, and it isn't, or it's not going to work, and it's not necessary.

resources_assistance.real_response: I think that's. Needed. The nine-year-old lady really needs like a handyman or a contractor to come. There didn't know what to do. I'll take care of it. Send the bill to the fire department or whatever. That's I think that's what people I was trying to.

memory_seeds.description: I started working on our home mitigation by creating a patio in the front to meet the five-foot Zone Zero requirement while still looking attractive.
My husband and I spent about three weeks doing all the digging and paving ourselves to build a little patio in front of our house.
We got free bricks from somebody who was trying to get rid of them, which helped us save money on the patio project.
We asked a retired contractor who lives around the corner for advice on how to build the patio.
We spent a lot of time on Etsy looking at garden art and wind art to make the Zone Zero area look attractive, not just boring gravel.
We ordered large gravel and sand from local rock and sand companies as a base for the bricks, and they delivered it easily.
I wanted to experiment and understand how much it costs to do this work even when being as economical as possible and doing it all yourself.
I realized that even when doing everything yourself, it still costs money unless you just weed five feet around your house and leave it as dirt.
We figured out that even if we did all the gate and fence installation ourselves, it would still cost a few thousand dollars just for materials.
I heard that neighbors who put in a metal fence just spent upwards of thirty thousand dollars, just for the fence.
I know some other neighbors further down the street put in a more attractive looking fence and spent even more than thirty thousand dollars.
The retired contractor neighbor showed us a kind of material he's going to use for part of a fence that I hadn't heard of before, and he was working with a local lumber company to carry it.
The retired contractor neighbor has done a really nice job on his house, and it looks great all the way around as a good example of compliant and attractive work.
We Googled how to build a patio and found websites and YouTube videos that all said the same thing, which made it straightforward.
I haven't personally tried to hire contractors for mitigation work because we've been doing stuff ourselves.
I heard reports that any time word gets out about a contractor who's knowledgeable about home hardening or fire-safe landscaping, they get so many calls that they become hard to get.
The general impression I get about needing to do fencing or gatework is that it's intimidating, there aren't that many options, and they all seem expensive.
I heard that someone attended a UC Berkeley landscape architecture webinar on Zone Zero ideas, but it seemed designed for flat suburban properties and didn't address hillside properties.
By some miracle, we have not lost our insurance yet, though it has gone up in cost.
The vast majority of people I talk to have said that they've had to switch insurance or go on the Fair Plan or something.
I heard that insurance companies may be legally prohibited from saying they'll pull all insurance from a zip code, so there has to be some randomness to it.
I know people who've lost their insurance even after they've done a fair amount of work and their homes look pretty good.
I also know other people who have kept their insurance despite not having done much work.
I think everybody here would like it if there was some kind of law that if you meet the fire department's basic standards, you're required and deserve to be insured.
I know insurance was a huge motivator in starting the process of becoming a Firewise community.
I think if there was a big enough carrot about getting cheaper insurance if you do this work, it would be a big incentive for people to cooperate with fire safety.
We ordered free gutter screens and attic vent materials from the Berkeley Fire Department, but most of the screens are still sitting in our garage because it took a long time to install them.
I've had people ask me if I know who can install the free gutter screens and attic vents from the fire department.
I suspect the fire department has grants that are specific to helping with vegetation management, but that's not what people really need help with.
I heard about Greg Murphy's neighbor in her nineties who wants to make changes but can't easily get started, and he's been helping her.

neighbor_pressure.real_response: I am outside every single day, raking up leaves every single day. I never see a single soul in my block doing that.

firewise_outreach.real_response: I think that they are mostly preaching to the choir. In other words, it's people that are already converted. But they're not gonna change anybody's mind.

resources_assistance.real_response: The guy that organized that understood that it was just a one-time cleanup and that the homeowners would then take over. Well, the homeowners aren't taking it out.
resources_assistance.context: Discussing student volunteer cleanup initiatives and the expectation that homeowners would maintain compliance afterward

memory_seeds.description: I went on Nextdoor and found an engineer who helped someone in Oakland put in an aluminum fence, and he's coming tomorrow to estimate how much permit plans will cost.

I believe the permit process for decks and maybe even for gates needs to be streamlined, and there should be a process within the Berkeley permit department where people going to zone zero should get preferential treatment in processing building permits.
My neighbors have 47 eucalyptus trees and every single day my house is littered with highly flammable debris, and there's nothing I can do to control it.
I live on Wildcat Canyon right across the street from East Bay MUD property, and I'm glad they're doing remediation, but they're not communicating what they're doing to residents.

I think if there was more information about what East Bay MUD is doing given to us, it would be welcome and invigorating and motivating, because I look across the street and know that if there's a fire over there, we're all goners.
State Farm canceled me last year, and my agent directed me to the California program with a dick policy that covers what the California FAIR policy doesn't cover.
The total cost of both insurance policies, not including earthquake insurance, is $1,000 a year.
There was one day in October of 2024 when there was an advisory to preemptively evacuate the Berkeley Hills, and no one evacuated but me.
People do not take the fire risk seriously, and the only thing that's going to make people comply is enforcement.
I found Firewise Communities very responsive and supportive when I reached out to them.

I know that the fire department is being very nice and they're not gonna enforce the ordinance in any significant way, and I understand why they're going to do that, but that's the only thing that's gonna make people comply.
About a thousand yards from my house in Tilden Park there is an open fire pit, and there are open fire pits all over Tilden Park directly downhill from my house where you can see flames.
I was sentimental about my plants too, but I'm also sentimental about not burning to death.
You have a highly entitled population here with no community spirit who want to do what they please to do, so this nice approach that Berkeley is taking is very nice but it's not going to work.
It's not fair to the people that are compliant after all the effort and money we've put in.
Berkeley has to take the nice approach because otherwise they're going to recall the fire chief and people are going to protest, it's all this entitlement.
They've made peace with these eucalyptus trees and I think the reason is because they're so costly to remove, but they maintain they're safe as long as they're limbed up.

The problem with limbing eucalyptus trees up is that with high winds, which is exactly the conditions for a fire, all of what is up comes down, so places that are completely litter-free need only about one hour of wind and they're covered in dead, oily, flammable eucalyptus debris.
My roof, which is metal and foam and not flammable, with a bout of wind gets covered in flammable eucalyptus material.
I changed my roof about 7 years ago because I was concerned about wildfire risk.
The roof cost sixty thousand dollars just for the roof.
I had to research the foam roof for months and months to find out what would work on my house.
I'm still doing research on the decking material and I do my research on the internet.
I've spent weeks researching materials and I would like to be able to walk on an aluminum deck and see what it feels like before committing.
There is one person in our neighborhood who put on an aluminum gate, and I go walking and can see it and it's beautiful, so having access to places that have made improvements helps a lot.
Zone zero is just 950 houses within our neighborhood, so it's helpful if people who make changes are promoted so other people considering changes can go and see what they've done.
Every single time you want to make a change you need to go through the permit process again and it's painful if it's not streamlined.
I think it's just a question of time before there's a fire, and you have a lot of older people living up here which increases the fire risk because they put their kettle on and don't turn it off.
You have people smoking marijuana across the street in the park, people living in the park, open fires in the park, and people throwing cigarettes out, so it's just a question of time.
I didn't get any grants for the changes I made to my home.

memory_seeds.description: I escaped my house during a fire in 2004, which was a formative experience.
In 2019, I was an Ember ordinance opponent who didn't believe in wildfire mitigation requirements despite my 2004 experience.
I had researched fire-resistant plants and planted them all within five feet of my house to hide it, and near my neighbors' homes because I didn't want to see them either.
I spent a year and a half in my off time reading hundreds of wildfire science articles and talking to the top 10 researchers in the field personally.
After a few weeks of research, but really after a year and a half when I had passed my psychological blockage, it became clear to me that wildfire mitigation totally makes sense.
Four years ago when my neighbor Luke bought the house next door, I told him we needed to take out all the shrubs between our houses because we only had 14 feet between them.
Luke told me I was crazy and said his wife was raised with the fire chief and would just call him to make inspections go away.
Now Luke and I have taken out every single shrub between our two houses and built a shared metal fence together.
Luke has put the best roof on his house for wildfire, a metal roof, and replaced all his windows with double-paned windows.
My neighbor behind me with a wood shingled home is having the grandfather fund wildfire proofing - metal roof, concrete board siding, new windows, and zone zero work.
My neighbor kitty corner was in a Japanese deportation camp in 1941 and is very old and poor now.
Last April we approached my poor elderly neighbor and asked if we could get volunteers to do all his zone zero maintenance, and he was very happy to let us.
My elderly neighbor's property used to be a nightmare of overgrowth but now it's zone zero compliant and super safe.
I co-founded Help Berkeley, a nonprofit, on the day of the shelter-in-place order in 2020 to deliver food to seniors who couldn't go to shops.
At the peak, Help Berkeley was delivering about 175,000 free meals per year in Berkeley and the East Bay.
Our neighbors on Creston have worked together for 30 plus years on earthquake preparation before we started on wildfire.
A year and a half ago we decided to focus our neighborhood efforts on wildfire prevention.
Yesterday we had a super successful meeting on gates and fences with eight vendors, expecting no more than 100 families but probably had 180-190 attendees and ran out of handouts.
Our Firewise group has 25 organizers and block captains for 108 houses, which is a very high ratio compared to other groups.
There's another Firewise group leader who basically gives orders and people don't like it, so she's lost her council twice and is starting from scratch again.
This morning someone from the other group's council who I was supposed to train sent an email saying they were too busy and couldn't do it.
We're doing an affordability study right now that will be out by the end of the month showing exactly what mitigation costs people on average.
My expectation is that for the average home, basic mitigation will cost less than $5,000, though some houses will cost more.
We did a study of 65 houses to count how many trees would be lost, and found only 5% of trees would need to be cut down.
The 5% of trees that need cutting are only in very specific situations where two houses are so close together there's no space for safe foliage between them.
We calculated that we're only going to lose about three and a quarter acres of vegetation at most in the Grizzly Peak mitigation area, assuming zero replacements.
Every April we do a big volunteer project where we find people who need help and bring hundreds of UC Berkeley students and residents to do work over a weekend.
In April we'll probably have three to four hundred volunteers working one weekend in the hills, many of them from UC Berkeley.
When I did my PhD at Caltech while working full time, it took me eight years and I lived on Chestnut and Virginia near North Berkeley BART as a tenant.

memory_seeds.description: I moved into this house about five years ago in the Berkeley Hills, right on Wildcat Canyon Road next to Tilden Park.
When I moved in, the previous owner told me they paid three thousand dollars a year for homeowners insurance with Allstate and gave me the agent's name.
I contacted Allstate and they just laughed in my face, saying they would never insure this house again.
Since moving in, I've never been able to get any of the big insurance companies to cover me.
Every year I get a different insurer through a broker who goes to the bespoke insurance market, all companies I've never heard of.
For the last three years I've been on California FAIR Plan with a wrapper because there's literally no one who will insure me.
My insurance peaked at eighteen thousand dollars a year but went down this year to fifteen thousand.
When I lived just over the hill in Kensington, I was paying three or four thousand a year for insurance, which was very reasonable.
I have a great broker based in Albany who proactively looks into the market every year knowing that the company is just going to drop me.
I've passed my insurance broker on to all my neighbors because they have to do the same dance with bespoke insurers.

I had confusion about what counts as 'the house' when removing vegetation within five feet, especially with my deck that goes from an upper level to a lower level and under the house, and a wooden trellis attached to the deck.
I had a landscaping company come in and told them point blank to remove everything within five feet of the house, and they did that.
When the fire inspection came, it turned out the landscapers didn't interpret it the same way the inspectors did, so I had to go in and remove some more things.
The inspectors inspect when you're not home and then leave you a little slip, so it's hard to talk to somebody.
The second round of inspections gave me different recommendations than the first round.
I did everything the first round said, and the second one came and found something else I was deficient in, so now I'm working on that.
I keep getting dinged on having wood attached to the house, even though it is Class A fire rated wood, which according to the ordinance is allowed.
I've worked with the same landscaper for 15 years, they're Planted Earth Construction based in the area and they live in the Hills in Kensington.
The landscaper did his best with the Ember work, but I had to do so much manual labor afterwards because they removed the plants but didn't remove all the mulch.
I had five years of Redwood mulch called gorilla fur that was 6 to 12 inches thick in places, and I had to take all of it out to get down to bare earth or stone near the house.
I suspect I will probably have 10 to 20K of expenses this year just to comply with Ember, and I've already spent probably seven thousand.
My immediate neighbor next door came to me to try to borrow money because he can't afford the homeowner work he needs to do.
Several neighbors want work done that they can't afford to do, like taking down several big eucalyptus trees that are 70 or 80 feet tall.
For a while within our Firewise mailing list, there was a very coordinated disinformation campaign from those who did not like the ordinance and didn't want it to be here.
The disinformation people were putting leaflets all over the neighborhood saying the ordinance means certain things, but it didn't mean that, they were just scaremongering.
There are so many houses here with beautiful landscaping within the zone where they have to have it removed, and it's going to completely change the feel of their house.
My neighbor Mori Morimoto is an architect who designed my house, his house, and our other neighbor's house, and he's been doing fire hardening for 10 years, since before it was cool.
Mori's whole house is surrounded by stone and river rock, done in a really beautiful way, but he's had people working on his house constantly for five years, maybe three days a week.
I've seen what goes into that level of hardening, and it's just such a labor intensive and money intensive process.
I went to the assistant fire chief for Berkeley when he gave talks, and I told him I've got the will, the time, and the money, just tell me clearly what to do and I will go do it.
Walking the dog, I've seen a lot of people out there with welders replacing all their fences with metal, three different houses in the last two weeks doing this right now.
Every week there's a battalion of trucks from tree companies blocking a road because they're taking down huge trees or dead trees.
There's a lot of great work being done in Tilden Park just to my left, I hear chainsaws all the time and they're doing such good work clearing out the brush and taking down trees.
The goats come by twice a year to clear out the underbrush in the area.
Now that I've ripped out all the plants, weeds are just sprouting up everywhere because there's no ground cover, so I've got to get that under control with stone.
The fire department leaves a hanger on your doorknob with a code after inspections, and you go to a website called defensible space report dot org to see what to do with photos.
Even the online platform with photos is open to interpretation because they just list the language of the ordinance and show a photo, and I'm left wondering what exactly they want me to do.

memory_seeds.description: My home was built in 2011 and finished after 2008, making it the only home in the neighborhood built after the latest fire code came into force.
I saw one of the fire maps that shows all the homes in the neighborhood, and there's one little tiny blue speck in the middle representing homes built after 2008, and that's our house.
Coming into compliance was fairly easy for us because we were already almost completely compliant with the Zone Zero requirements.
I received a notice in the driveway or mailbox about the inspection, then went online and made an appointment with the fire inspector people.
Two nice young ladies showed up for the inspection and gave us a thorough inspection and then they went away.
We just had an inspection and were fully in compliance with no violations.
The inspectors made a couple of comments and suggestions during the inspection, but it was very easy for us.
We didn't have one of the many problematic homes in the neighborhood where landscaping has been going on for 100 years and no one's ever taken down a bush.
We had nothing growing next to the house—we have concrete and gravel five feet around the house everywhere.
We use Chubb insurance, which basically insures high-end homes and contents and has always charged substantially more than other insurance companies for superior coverage.
I understand that Chubb is not taking on any new coverage in the area, but they just renewed us fairly recently.
I'm assuming that so far, so good with insurance—until they don't renew, you're okay.
The house is built with what they call Class A siding, which is one-hour fire-resistant siding, and it's got a Class A one-hour resistant roof.
The house is all concrete on the bottom, there are no eaves, no wooden decks, and it's completely sprinklered—it's a modern home.
Almost all the community is much older than 2008—homes around here were built in the 20s, 30s, 40s, and 70s.
Many homeowners are going to have to make difficult choices about vegetation removal, and in our case the choices weren't difficult, so we were fortunate.
In many cases homeowners have felt that they're just not going to comply with the regulations.
Homeowners give aesthetic reasons for not complying—they don't want to lose their wisteria or camellia that's been here for 50 years and is beautiful, which is a perfectly good reason.

If the camellia causes their house to burn down and their house burning down causes the next house to burn down, that's where city and state regulations are going to come into play, similar to mandatory seismic upgrades over the past 20 or 30 years.
Compliance will be difficult and very expensive for many people—there's just no question about it.
Having their house burnt down will be much more difficult and much more expensive—you could have millions of dollars of losses as opposed to $10,000 or $20,000 of expense at this time.
Some people don't want to spend the $10,000 or $20,000, and I can understand that, but that's why you have laws and compliance becomes enforceable.
People are more and more aware of the fire problem, and you do see a lot of homeowners making some effort.
Coming to full compliance can be very difficult, and I don't hold a lot of hope out that full compliance will be reached in the entire community.
Homes are pretty compliant with seismic retrofitting standards that came into force since the '89 Loma Prieta quake, so maybe that'll happen with fire regulations also.
Enforcement and penalties will always be effective, and insurance companies will drive much of the compliance.
If you can't insure your home without being compliant, people will comply—if you can't insure your home, you can't sell it, you can't buy it, you can't do anything.
A huge amount of progress has been made in the past year or so since the Palisades fires—people are talking about it all the time and it's a major concern.
Firewise groups are all over Berkeley, especially in the hills from south to north, and they seem to be very active, contacting almost every single homeowner.
The fire department is obviously contacting every single household, so we're getting there with compliance efforts.

memory_seeds.description: I represented the Northeast Berkeley Hills on the city council for 16 years, from Hearst Avenue all the way up to the reservoir at the top of Spruce and Grizzly Peak, entirely in the very high hazard wildfire area.
I learned that in 1923, more than 100 years ago, we had a very bad wildfire in North Berkeley that spread all the way down to Shattuck and Hearst, losing 625 structures.
I saw in 1991 the Berkeley-Oakland Tunnel Fire, which was at that time the worst fire in the history of wildfires in the United States, losing more than 2,000 structures and 25 lives.
I learned from the 1991 fire that fire departments in individual jurisdictions were not coordinated—their radio frequencies couldn't communicate and Berkeley hoses didn't work on Oakland hydrants.

I used the 2017 Tubbs fire in Santa Rosa to convince my council colleagues that wildfire is a citywide issue, not just a hills issue, because embers were blowing to areas nowhere near high-fire zones.
I helped pass Measure FF in 2020, which brought nine million dollars a year into the fire department, with about two million going to wildfire mitigation, through a unanimous city council vote.
I worked with people from Oakland to form the East Bay Wildfire Coalition of Governments because wildfire doesn't know geographic boundaries and we needed a regional approach.
I was the founding chair of the East Bay Wildfire Coalition of Governments, bringing together two counties and all jurisdictions along the ridge from Hercules to Oakland.

I brought in UC Berkeley, Lawrence Lab, East Bay Regional Parks, and East Bay Municipal Utility District as advisory partners so everyone would be in the same room hearing and sharing the same information.
I identified two specific areas as most vulnerable based on fire science modeling: east of Grizzly Peak adjacent to Tilden Park with about 900 homes, and Panoramic Hill above the stadium.
I observed that Panoramic Hill is only one way in and one way out with very narrow and curvy roads, making it kind of like a death trap.
I started educating my constituents about wildfire safety and educating my colleagues on the city council for 16 years.
I encountered a political division where people felt that wildfire was a hills issue that only affected rich white people in the hills, not everybody else.
I heard from constituents at annual community meetings and workshops on fire safety that were attended by maybe 250 people, even during Zoom, because people care.
I helped establish 23 certified Firewise communities in Berkeley through those workshops, with several more in the pipeline working on certification.

I learned that the Firewise certification process requires identifying geographic boundaries, doing an inventory of all properties, evaluating compliance, and creating a three-year work plan that Cal Fire reviews and often sends back for corrections.
I observed that luckily in Berkeley we have a very engaged community with very generous people, and retired people who have the time to take on Firewise leadership.
I've been frustrated by naysayers on social media saying that what we're doing is wrong and the science is wrong, people who just won't accept it.
I know that Professor Golna at UC Berkeley is one of the leaders in providing the science behind fire mitigation measures.
I've encountered tree huggers who are loud and passionate about their beliefs, thinking it's more important to have plants strike up against your house or that plants need to be protected.
I recognized that what we are asking people to do does cost money, and we need to figure out a way to incentivize the work and support those who are willing but don't have the money.
I see that Berkeley's landscape is very iconic, famous for its brown shingle homes with trellises of wisteria from the craftsman look of the 1920s, and we're asking people to change that aesthetic.
I feel the burden falls on us to provide people with options of how it might look instead, as part of the education campaign.
I thought that to spend $10,000 on a $2 million house doesn't seem like a big ask from my standpoint, given that a home in Berkeley is your largest financial investment.
I learned that for new construction, there are state laws and updated building codes, so any new construction will be very fire safe.
I observed that Berkeley is a built environment without a lot of new construction in the hills, so it's the existing houses that are of concern and we have to retrofit them.
I helped create Ember ordinance legislation for zone zero restrictions before I retired a little bit more than a year ago.
I learned that inspections are funded by Measure FF and are currently focusing on that narrow area on the other side of Grizzly Peak, taking it a little bit at a time.
I know the city provides free chippers, free green bin dumpsters for neighborhood cleanups, and chipper service that turns tree limbs into sawdust and recycles it.

I observed that the state is passing zone zero regulations but dragging their feet, and it's an unfunded mandate with nothing coming down from the state to residents and no funding coming into cities to support it.

memory_seeds.description: I moved into this house in 2001 with my husband and two children, and we've been here ever since.
After the LA fires, we felt a lot of anxiety and it felt very imperative to do something in response to fire risk.
I work as an attorney, though I work less now than I did before, and I've always maintained my career even after having children.

I tried to work with Jennifer because as an attorney I work with everyone, even clients I don't like, but I finally realized there's no way to work with her.

I've had my kids in UC Berkeley daycare for five years, plus we had an au pair for 10 years to help with pickup and drop-off.

State Farm had insured our home since 2001, and before that they covered our renters insurance in the flats, so we'd been with them over 30 years.
State Farm told us they would not renew our policy when it expired, and we got non-renewal notice last May.

Our old State Farm policy cost $5,000, but the Fair Plan quoted $15,000 for a $3 million policy that would actually only pay out about a million dollars because of the age discount on our 30-something-year-old roof.
Our roof is a clay tile roof that cost at least $150,000 to install and is supposed to last 75 years, but the Fair Plan only cares that it's 36 years old.

I decided not to buy the Fair Plan policy and instead bought insurance from a company outside California that isn't regulated by the state insurance commissioner, paying about five times what I paid before.
One of my neighbors on Creston had his insurance on do-not-renew, then did a whole lot of cutting and got his insurance reinstated.
